news_summary: Innovative natural solutions are advancing agriculture, significantly reducing diesel pollution, promoting sustainable urban greening, and uncovering beneficial plant-insect relationships.
news_summary_fr: Des solutions naturelles innovantes font progresser l'agriculture, réduisent considérablement la pollution due au diesel, favorisent la végétalisation urbaine durable et mettent en lumière les relations bénéfiques entre les plantes et les insectes.
news_summary_es: Las soluciones naturales innovadoras están impulsando la agricultura, reduciendo considerablemente la contaminación por diésel, fomentando la ecologización urbana sostenible y sacando a la luz relaciones beneficiosas entre plantas e insectos.

articles.title: How the humble hornwort could supercharge agriculture
articles.content:
You are here because of a single, all-important enzyme. But don’t look inward to find ribulose-­1,5-­bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase, known more mercifully among scientists as rubisco. Instead, look to the food you eat and the trees that manufacture oxygen, as this is the protein that makes photosynthesis possible. Without it, life on Earth as we know it would not exist.

For all that heavy lifting, rubisco is remarkably inefficient. The enzyme converts carbon dioxide into sugars that sustain plants. But it is easily confused, and will react with oxygen in a process that creates a toxic byproduct, wastes energy, and limits how quickly plants can grow. (Which is not to fault rubisco: This is a very difficult reaction to pull off, and just look at how many plants have been getting it right for hundreds of millions of years.) That includes the essential crops that feed humanity — grains, vegetables, fruits — that could theoretically grow better if their rubisco worked more efficiently. This problem becomes more urgent as global temperatures rise, because this essential protein gets even less efficient in the heat.

Enter the hornwort. This tiny plant, which is related to mosses, grows as a green sheet on the ground. It’s the only known land plant that’s found a way (evolutionarily speaking) to supercharge rubisco by concentrating CO2 around the enzyme.

Now, an international team of scientists says it has figured out how hornwort does this, and how it may be able to apply that superpower to crops. That could mean massively improved yields, so farmers wouldn’t need to cultivate as much land to grow the same amount of food. “It’s very impressive,” said Robert Wilson, a biochemist who studies rubisco at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. (Wilson wasn’t involved in the research but does collaborate with one of the scientists.) “It’s interesting, because it’s a completely new and novel mechanism through which an important aspect of rubisco biochemistry occurs.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Scientists have long known that some species of algae also improve the efficiency of rubisco. Inside their chloroplasts — the structures within cells where photosynthesis hums along — they’ve developed specialized compartments known as pyrenoids. These concentrate CO2 around the enzyme, minimizing its reaction with oxygen. “It prevents rubisco from touching oxygen, because it puts it into a house and then pumps a bunch of CO2 into the house,” said Laura Gunn, a synthetic plant biologist at Cornell University and coauthor of a new paper describing the work. “So the rubisco is completely, completely saturated with CO2, and all the oxygen is outside the house.”

But because algae are so distantly related to the foods we eat, it would be challenging to genetically modify crops to mimic those pyrenoids. On the other hand, the hornwort is more closely related. These researchers discovered that it has a unique way of making these pyrenoid structures, due to a protein they’re calling RbcS-STAR. Rubisco in all plants is made of proteins, but the hornwort’s version has an extra “tail,” which helps tether the enzymes together to create compartments into which CO2 pumps, increasing the efficiency of the process.

To that end, the researchers introduced the RbcS-STAR protein into a closely related hornwort species that doesn’t have pyrenoids, and sure enough its rubisco reorganized to create those compartments. They then did the same with Arabidopsis — a small flowering plant commonly used in lab experiments as a model organism — and it too responded. “They form pyrenoid-like structures, and that will be a very important step toward engineering a better photosynthesis using this type of CO2-concentrating mechanism,” said Fay-Wei Li, a plant biologist at Cornell University and the Boyce Thompson Institute and coauthor of the paper. Now the team is looking to do the same by genetically modifying crops: Gunn said that by adding pyrenoid structures, researchers might boost growth and yields by as much as 60 percent.

At the moment, though, the researchers have so far just built the pyrenoid-like house. “Basically, we’ve built the walls, right, we’ve built the roof,” Gunn said. “But we haven’t got the HVAC system yet. We haven’t got the system in there that’s going to pump the CO2 in, and then going to pump out the sugar products at the end.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Once the researchers figure that out, it could mean a field day for farmers the world over. Because Rubisco is so inefficient, plants must produce a lot of it. To encourage that, farmers have to apply heaps of synthetic fertilizers. All those chemicals are not only expensive, but also terrible for the environment: It takes an immense amount of energy to produce, then pollutes waterways when it runs off fields.

And speaking of water, the breakthrough could also mean crops use less of it. Plants are dotted with little structures called stomata, which open up so they can “inhale” CO2. Because rubisco is so inefficient, plants need to breathe deeply to get enough of the gas, but that releases more water vapor through the stomata, hence having to constantly irrigate crops. “If a rubisco does not react with oxygen as much, the plants can close their stomata more often,” Li said. “If you can close your stomata, then you will not lose as much water.”

But if algae and the hornwort have figured out how to improve rubisco, why haven’t all plants? Algae live in aquatic environments, where CO2 dissolves poorly, so they’ve had to make the best of what little they’ve got. For the hornworts, the reason isn’t actually clear, especially since related species living in the same environment haven’t evolved pyrenoids. As for all the other land plants lacking a better system, one reason might be that rubisco evolved at a time when there wasn’t much oxygen in the atmosphere, meaning plants didn’t have to worry about the enzyme getting distracted as it tried to process CO2.

All these years later, scientists have cracked the code, and they could soon make rubisco even more of an essential enzyme. “I think it’s very likely that improvements to crop yields through plant synthetic biology will be forthcoming in the next 10 years,” Wilson said. “And that’s very exciting, because that’s where the field has been trying to go for many decades now.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding and potentially improving photosynthesis efficiency in crops by mimicking a natural CO2-concentrating mechanism found in hornworts. This advancement could lead to substantially higher crop yields, reduced need for fertilizers, and lower water usage, benefiting global agriculture and the environment on a large scale. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful positive impact for society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: Scientists unlock plant enzyme boost from tiny hornwort to improve crops
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Plants need an enzyme called rubisco to turn carbon dioxide into sugars through photosynthesis. But rubisco is slow and gets confused by oxygen, which wastes energy and limits plant growth. This problem worsens with rising temperatures and affects important food crops.

**What happened** – Scientists studied a small plant called hornwort, which has a special way to make rubisco work better. Hornwort creates tiny compartments called pyrenoids that concentrate carbon dioxide around rubisco, helping it work faster and avoid oxygen. Researchers found a protein called RbcS-STAR that helps form these compartments. They added this protein to other plants, including a common lab plant named Arabidopsis, and those plants started making pyrenoid-like structures.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a new method to improve photosynthesis in crops, potentially increasing food production by up to 60%. It could also reduce farmers’ need for fertilizers and water, which are costly and harm the environment. Plants with better rubisco would lose less water and grow faster, helping agriculture become more efficient.

**What's next step** – Scientists still need to add the system that pumps carbon dioxide into the pyrenoid compartments and moves sugars out. Once complete, they plan to introduce this technology into actual crop plants to boost their growth and yields.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have taken a key step toward making crops grow faster and use resources better by copying a tiny plant’s natural trick to improve a vital enzyme.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques décryptent l’astuce d’une petite plante pour améliorer les cultures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les plantes ont besoin d’une enzyme appelée rubisco pour transformer le dioxyde de carbone en sucres grâce à la photosynthèse. Mais cette enzyme est lente et peut se tromper avec l’oxygène, ce qui gaspille de l’énergie et limite la croissance des plantes. Ce problème s’aggrave avec la montée des températures et touche les cultures alimentaires importantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié une petite plante appelée hornwort, qui possède une méthode spéciale pour rendre la rubisco plus efficace. Le hornwort crée de petits compartiments appelés pyrenoïdes qui concentrent le dioxyde de carbone autour de la rubisco, l’aidant à mieux fonctionner et à éviter l’oxygène. Les chercheurs ont identifié une protéine nommée RbcS-STAR qui aide à former ces compartiments. Ils ont ajouté cette protéine à d’autres plantes, dont une plante modèle appelée Arabidopsis, qui ont alors commencé à fabriquer des structures semblables aux pyrenoïdes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une nouvelle façon d’améliorer la photosynthèse dans les cultures, ce qui pourrait augmenter la production alimentaire jusqu’à 60%. Cela pourrait aussi réduire le besoin d’engrais et d’eau des agriculteurs, qui sont coûteux et nocifs pour l’environnement. Les plantes avec une rubisco améliorée perdraient moins d’eau et pousseraient plus vite, rendant l’agriculture plus efficace.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques doivent encore ajouter le système qui pompe le dioxyde de carbone dans les pyrenoïdes et évacue les sucres. Une fois cela réalisé, ils prévoient d’introduire cette technologie dans les cultures pour stimuler leur croissance et leurs rendements.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont franchi une étape clé pour faire pousser les cultures plus vite et mieux utiliser les ressources en imitant l’astuce naturelle d’une petite plante pour améliorer une enzyme vitale.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo una planta pequeña mejora enzima clave para cultivos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las plantas necesitan una enzima llamada rubisco para convertir el dióxido de carbono en azúcares mediante la fotosíntesis. Pero la rubisco es lenta y se confunde con el oxígeno, lo que desperdicia energía y limita el crecimiento de las plantas. Este problema empeora con el aumento de las temperaturas y afecta cultivos alimentarios importantes.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron una planta pequeña llamada hornwort, que tiene una forma especial de hacer que la rubisco funcione mejor. El hornwort crea pequeños compartimentos llamados pirenoides que concentran dióxido de carbono alrededor de la rubisco, ayudándola a trabajar más rápido y evitar el oxígeno. Los investigadores encontraron una proteína llamada RbcS-STAR que ayuda a formar estos compartimentos. Añadieron esta proteína a otras plantas, incluyendo una planta común en laboratorios llamada Arabidopsis, y estas plantas comenzaron a crear estructuras similares a pirenoides.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra un método nuevo para mejorar la fotosíntesis en cultivos, lo que podría aumentar la producción de alimentos hasta en un 60%. También podría reducir la necesidad de fertilizantes y agua, que son caros y dañan el medio ambiente. Las plantas con rubisco mejorada perderían menos agua y crecerían más rápido, ayudando a que la agricultura sea más eficiente.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos aún deben agregar el sistema que bombea dióxido de carbono dentro de los pirenoides y mueve los azúcares fuera. Cuando esto esté listo, planean introducir esta tecnología en cultivos reales para aumentar su crecimiento y rendimiento.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos han dado un paso clave para hacer que los cultivos crezcan más rápido y usen mejor los recursos copiando el truco natural de una planta pequeña para mejorar una enzima vital.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a lush green hornwort plant with its unique molecular "house" structure glowing softly around clusters of enzymes, symbolizing the pyrenoid compartments concentrating carbon dioxide, set against a gentle abstract background of growing crops subtly shaped like interconnected leaves, illustrating the promise of enhanced photosynthesis and abundant harvests.

articles.title: Simple water trick cuts diesel engine pollution by over 60%
articles.summary: Scientists are exploring a surprisingly simple way to clean up diesel engines: adding tiny droplets of water to the fuel. During combustion, the water rapidly vaporizes, triggering micro-explosions that improve fuel mixing and lower combustion temperatures. Studies show this technique can slash nitrogen oxide and soot emissions by more than 60% while sometimes even improving engine efficiency. Because it works in existing engines without redesign, it could provide a quick path to cleaner diesel use.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Federal University of Technology Owerri in Nigeria have identified a promising strategy for reducing pollution from diesel engines without hurting their performance. By analyzing studies from around the world, the team examined a technology known as Water-in-Diesel Emulsion (WiDE). Their findings suggest that adding small amounts of water to diesel fuel could sharply lower harmful emissions while maintaining, or even improving, how efficiently engines operate.

Diesel engines play an important role in transportation, agriculture, and industrial equipment because they are reliable and capable of delivering strong power. At the same time, they are a significant source of air pollution. Diesel exhaust contains nitrogen oxides and particulate matter, pollutants linked to smog, respiratory disease, and climate impacts. Technologies such as catalytic converters and particulate filters can help reduce these emissions, but they also increase the cost and complexity of engines. The researchers suggest that WiDE could offer a simpler and cleaner option that works in existing diesel engines without requiring modifications.

How Water-in-Diesel Emulsion Technology Works

WiDE technology mixes extremely small droplets of water into diesel fuel. Special chemicals called surfactants keep the water and diesel evenly blended, allowing the emulsion to remain stable for up to sixty days.

When this fuel burns inside the engine, the water droplets rapidly turn into vapor. This rapid vaporization triggers a phenomenon known as a "micro-explosion," which helps break up the fuel into finer particles. As a result, air and fuel mix more thoroughly during combustion. This improved mixing lowers peak combustion temperatures, which reduces the formation of nitrogen oxides. At the same time, more complete burning of the fuel decreases soot and particulate emissions.

Major Reductions in Diesel Engine Emissions

According to studies reviewed in the analysis, engines running on WiDE can significantly reduce harmful pollutants. Nitrogen oxide emissions dropped by as much as sixty-seven percent, while particulate matter declined by up to sixty-eight percent compared with standard diesel fuel.

In addition to cutting pollution, several experiments reported improvements in brake thermal efficiency. This measure reflects how effectively an engine converts fuel into useful mechanical power. In other words, the engines not only produced cleaner exhaust but also used fuel more efficiently.

"Water-in-diesel emulsions are a practical and cost-effective way to make diesel engines cleaner," said lead author Dr. Chukwuemeka Fortunatus Nnadozie. "Because the technology does not require redesigning the engine, it offers an immediate path toward lower emissions in developing and developed countries alike."

The Role of Surfactants in Fuel Stability

The researchers also explored the importance of surfactants, the compounds that allow water and diesel to stay evenly mixed. Choosing the right surfactant and using the correct concentration are essential for keeping the emulsion stable. Stability is important because it affects both the safety and performance of the fuel.

Their review found that mixtures using multiple surfactants tended to provide the best results. These formulations improved both the stability of the fuel blend and the quality of combustion inside the engine.

A Potential Bridge to Cleaner Energy

Although the results are encouraging, the researchers say additional work is still needed. Future studies should focus on refining surfactant combinations and evaluating the long term effects of water diesel emulsions on engine components.

The team also notes that WiDE could work alongside other clean technologies. Combining this fuel approach with biodiesel and advanced emission control systems could help support broader climate and air quality goals.

"This technology can bridge the gap between conventional diesel use and a cleaner energy future," said co-author Professor Emeka Emmanuel Oguzie. "With proper formulation and testing, it could become an important part of sustainable transportation and industrial power systems."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a scientifically validated technology—Water-in-Diesel Emulsion (WiDE)—that can significantly reduce harmful diesel engine emissions by over 60%, improving air quality and potentially benefiting public health and climate. It describes a practical, cost-effective solution that can be implemented immediately in existing engines worldwide without redesign, making the impact broad and meaningful. The article provides detailed context on how the technology works, its benefits, and future research directions, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world environmental impact.
articles.personality_title: Adding water to diesel fuel cuts engine pollution by over 60%
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Diesel engines are widely used in transportation, farming, and factories because they are powerful and reliable. However, they produce harmful air pollution, including nitrogen oxides and soot, which can cause health problems and environmental damage. Current pollution controls can be costly and complex.

**What happened** – Scientists at the Federal University of Technology Owerri in Nigeria studied a method called Water-in-Diesel Emulsion (WiDE). This involves mixing tiny droplets of water into diesel fuel using special chemicals called surfactants. When the fuel burns, the water vaporizes quickly, causing small explosions that help the fuel burn more evenly and at lower temperatures.

**Impact** – Using WiDE can reduce nitrogen oxide emissions by up to 67% and soot by up to 68%. It can also make engines run more efficiently, meaning they use less fuel for the same power. Since this method works with existing engines without changes, it offers a simple and affordable way to cut pollution worldwide.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to improve the mix of surfactants to keep the water and fuel stable longer and study how WiDE affects engines over time. They also see potential for combining WiDE with other clean technologies like biodiesel to further reduce pollution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding tiny amounts of water to diesel fuel is a simple, cost-effective way to lower harmful emissions and improve engine efficiency without changing the engine itself.
articles.personality_title_fr: Ajouter de l'eau au diesel réduit la pollution des moteurs de plus de 60%
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les moteurs diesel sont largement utilisés dans les transports, l'agriculture et l'industrie car ils sont puissants et fiables. Cependant, ils produisent une pollution atmosphérique nocive, notamment des oxydes d'azote et des particules de suie, qui peuvent causer des problèmes de santé et des dégâts environnementaux. Les systèmes actuels de contrôle de la pollution sont souvent coûteux et complexes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques de l'Université Fédérale de Technologie Owerri au Nigeria ont étudié une méthode appelée émulsion eau-diesel (WiDE). Elle consiste à mélanger de minuscules gouttes d'eau dans le carburant diesel à l'aide de produits chimiques spéciaux appelés tensioactifs. Lorsque ce carburant brûle, l'eau s'évapore rapidement, provoquant de petites explosions qui aident le carburant à brûler plus uniformément et à des températures plus basses.

**Impact** – L'utilisation de WiDE peut réduire les émissions d'oxydes d'azote jusqu'à 67 % et la suie jusqu'à 68 %. Elle peut aussi améliorer l'efficacité des moteurs, c’est-à-dire qu’ils consomment moins de carburant pour la même puissance. Comme cette méthode fonctionne avec les moteurs existants sans modification, elle offre un moyen simple et abordable de réduire la pollution dans le monde entier.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs souhaitent améliorer la composition des tensioactifs pour que le mélange eau-carburant reste stable plus longtemps et étudier l'effet de WiDE sur les moteurs à long terme. Ils envisagent aussi de combiner WiDE avec d'autres technologies propres comme le biodiesel pour réduire encore plus la pollution.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Ajouter de petites quantités d'eau au diesel est une solution simple et économique pour réduire les émissions nocives et améliorer l'efficacité des moteurs sans les modifier.
articles.personality_title_es: Agregar agua al combustible diésel reduce la contaminación del motor en más del 60%
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los motores diésel se usan mucho en transporte, agricultura e industria porque son potentes y confiables. Sin embargo, producen contaminación dañina, como óxidos de nitrógeno y hollín, que afectan la salud y el medio ambiente. Los sistemas actuales para controlar esta contaminación suelen ser caros y complicados.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Federal de Tecnología Owerri en Nigeria estudiaron una técnica llamada emulsión agua-diésel (WiDE). Esta mezcla pequeñas gotas de agua en el combustible diésel usando sustancias especiales llamadas tensioactivos. Cuando el combustible se quema, el agua se vaporiza rápido, causando pequeñas explosiones que ayudan a que el combustible se mezcle mejor y queme a temperaturas más bajas.

**Impacto** – Usar WiDE puede reducir las emisiones de óxidos de nitrógeno hasta en un 67 % y el hollín hasta en un 68 %. También puede mejorar la eficiencia del motor, es decir, usar menos combustible para la misma potencia. Como esta técnica funciona en motores existentes sin cambios, ofrece una forma sencilla y económica de reducir la contaminación en todo el mundo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren mejorar la mezcla de tensioactivos para que el agua y el combustible se mantengan estables por más tiempo y estudiar cómo WiDE afecta a los motores a largo plazo. También ven potencial para combinar WiDE con otras tecnologías limpias como el biodiésel para reducir aún más la contaminación.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Agregar pequeñas cantidades de agua al combustible diésel es una forma simple y económica de reducir emisiones dañinas y mejorar la eficiencia del motor sin cambiar el motor.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vintage diesel engine silhouette gently surrounded by swirling droplets of water and diesel fuel merging seamlessly, with subtle vapor micro-explosions symbolized by delicate bursts of mist and light, set against a simple, natural-toned background that evokes clean air and reduced pollution.

articles.title: The race for Paris: Why France’s capital has likely gone green for good
articles.summary: With France preparing to vote in the first round of the municipal elections on Sunday, Paris has become a battleground between left- and right-wing forces fighting to shape the capital’s future. But while conservatives once fought tooth and nail against the left’s dream of a city of more trees and less traffic, a look through the rival programmes suggests a greener capital is here to stay.
articles.content:
A photo shows an artwork by US artist Shepard Fairey on the façade of the city hall and a new green space on the square, in Paris, on June 23, 2025.

If there’s one thing that every candidate in the city’s upcoming mayoral election seems to agree on, it’s this: for better or worse, a quarter-century of left-wing rule has changed Paris beyond recognition.

For the outgoing administration of Socialist Mayor Anne Hidalgo, the fruits of the city’s green transformation brook no argument.

Carbon emissions have dropped by almost a third since 2004, and major air pollutants by half over roughly the same period. A city known for its densely packed Hausmmannian apartment blocks has witnessed the planting of some 150,000 trees since 2020 as of March 2026 – though almost a third of these have apparently been replacements for those struck down over the years.

Perhaps the most obvious change, to visitors and locals alike, has been the heavy reduction in the amount of traffic heaving its way across the city: the number of cars in circulation has dropped by more than half since 2004, largely due to a series of policies by the city’s Socialist mayors to prioritise pedestrians and cyclists.

Nowhere does this transformation feel more complete than along the banks of the Seine that carves the city in two. More than a decade ago, Hidalgo’s predecessor Bertrand Delanoë banned cars from the winding river’s left bank for the first time since the 1960s.

People walk and rest on the Seine River banks in Paris on June 21, 2025. © Geoffroy van der Hasselt, AFP

The measure was fiercely opposed by the city’s right-wing opposition – including the mayor of the wealthy 7th arrondissement, Rachida Dati, who polls now put within striking distance of the Hôtel de Ville. In the years that followed, Dati and her allies continued to campaign against the pedestrianisation of the river’s right bank, the Pont d’Iena and swathes of the city centre.

Today’s Dati, by contrast, is unrecognisable. Preaching what she calls a “pragmatic environmentalism”, she is running on the promise to preserve Paris’s 200,000 trees, enrich the capital with 500 new green belts and create “oases of freshness” by stripping away asphalt in favour of grass-lined cobblestones. Above all, she says, she will put the Parisian pedestrian “at the heart of her campaign”.

Going green

The conservative former culture minister is not the only one on the right who has changed their tune on a greener Paris.

Far-right National Rally candidate Thierry Mariani has pledged to plant another 50,000 trees, in particular in Paris’s school courtyards. The promise to green the capital’s schools is shared by most of the other candidates, including the centre-right Pierre-Yves Bournazel, who also champions the revitalisation of the 36-kilometre “Petite ceinture” – a disused railway looping around Paris – with family-friendly green spaces and dog parks.

Even the steady pedestrianisation of Paris has found almost unanimous support among the different rivals. Once howled down by the right as overly hostile to cars and the people who drive them, the policy is now widely welcomed by Parisians – particularly the 300-odd streets in front of schools that have been restricted to pedestrians in the past five years. Bournazel has proposed extending the measure to 626 schools if elected.

Divided left battles rising far right as France heads to the polls To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again "On the left, the presidential battle already on everyone's mind", says Le Monde © France 24 04:56

Not to be outdone, Hidalgo’s former deputy Emmanuel Grégoire – the head of a left-wing coalition including the Greens and the Communist Party, and the narrow favourite ahead of the first round of voting Sunday – has said that he will create 1,000 new pedestrian-only streets and 300 new hectares of public gardens. His team has also outlined more sweeping changes including a leafy 25-kilometre promenade stretching across both banks of the Seine and a city-spanning green belt following Paris’s Périphérique ring road.

Hard-left candidate Sophia Chikirou is pushing for the greening of 40 percent of the capital’s territory by 2032 and the linking of Paris’s Bois de Boulogne and Bois de Vincennes by a green belt open to cyclists and pedestrians alike.

Only far-right candidate Sarah Knafo is bucking the trend. The 32-year-old is pledging to reopen the banks of the Seine to cars, raise the speed limit on the capital’s ring road back to 80km/h and drastically reduce the steep costs of parking your car in Paris. Even these measures, it must be said, are framed within the context of reducing the city’s environmental impact, with Knafo stressing that a car on the move pollutes three times less than one stuck in traffic.

A sense of crisis

Nathalie Blanc, director of research at the National Centre for Scientific research and director of the Earth Politics Centre, said that Parisians were largely convinced by the urgency imposed by the climate crisis.

“One of the first things we’ve seen is the acceleration of climate change and the population's acceptance of this narrative, as well as the need for collective adaptation – particularly in cities,” she said. “As recent work by the Shift Project [a think tank advocating a transition to a post-carbon economy] has shown, environmental integration and the need to take climate change into account are widely supported at the local level, in a context where national policies remain largely insufficient.”

Nor is the ongoing environmental collapse the distant or abstract threat it once seemed. Reeling from a series of worsening heat waves, city officials have recently begun to model how the city would cope once summer temperatures rise to 50 degrees – something that climate scientists warn is a real possibility over the next century. The answer was alarming, and unsurprising: not well.

Blanc said that environmental policies pushed by city officials were increasingly built not just around keeping carbon emissions down, but better preparing Paris to deal with the consequences of a warming world.

“In a context where adaptation remains the poor relation of climate policies even as greenhouse gas emission mitigation has benefited from considerable investment over the last few decades, some public policies have gone beyond the strict logic of mitigation – often perceived as a restrictive, even punitive, ecological injunction, and deemed ineffective given the scale of emissions from the Chinese and North American economies – to more explicitly integrate territorial adaptation issues.”

Quality-of-life

But while the climate crisis still looms large in the public imagination, many of the environmental measures put into place across France’s cities are rooted in more down-to-earth needs.

A report released by the Shift Project last month polling mayors and other municipal-level elected officials across France found that local representatives were increasingly framing environmental measures as concrete quality-of-life improvements for residents rather than a broader push away from fossil fuels.

More than three-quarters of those polled listed safeguarding their constituents’ health or quality of life as one of the main motivating factors of their support for environmental policies, with lowering their energy costs following closely behind. The report found overwhelming support among elected officials for practical measures including renovating apartments to make them more energy-efficient and investing in public transport or pedestrian-friendly infrastructure.

Read moreThe race for Paris: How the capital’s housing crisis could determine the city’s next mayor

“Certain measures stand out for their double effectiveness in terms of mitigation and adaptation,” Blanc said. “This is particularly the case for the greening of urban spaces, which is widely accepted by society, and the thermal renovation of buildings, which simultaneously contributes to reducing emissions, improving comfort, and increasing resilience to heat waves.”

But she stressed that not all of this support came from concern over how to best shield the city’s most vulnerable people from the extremes unleashed by the climate crisis.

“Beyond these specific considerations, the greening of cities also corresponds to a process of gentrification, to the extent that neighbourhoods bordering green spaces can see their value increase,” she said. “For wealthy property owners, this can be a reason to approve this type of development. But from a quality-of-life perspective, this greening of cities also brings with it a renewed focus on collective well-being.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and broad positive environmental transformation in Paris, with measurable reductions in carbon emissions and pollutants, large-scale tree planting, and pedestrian-friendly policies supported across political lines. These changes improve public health, quality of life, and climate resilience, reflecting meaningful progress in urban sustainability and climate adaptation.
articles.personality_title: Paris’s green transformation wins broad support ahead of elections
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Paris has been changing a lot over the past 25 years, especially under left-wing mayors who have focused on making the city greener and less crowded with cars. As municipal elections approach, candidates from different political parties are debating the city’s future.

**What happened** – While in the past conservatives opposed green policies like reducing car traffic and planting trees, many now support similar ideas. Paris has cut carbon emissions by almost a third and air pollution by half since 2004. About 150,000 trees have been planted since 2020, and car numbers have dropped by more than half. Candidates from the left, right, and far-right mostly agree on protecting and increasing green spaces and making the city more pedestrian-friendly.

**Impact** – This wide agreement on environmental goals shows that Paris’s green changes are likely to continue. These policies help reduce pollution, improve health, and prepare the city for hotter summers caused by climate change. The focus on green spaces also improves daily life by creating nicer public areas and safer streets near schools.

**What's next step** – The upcoming election will decide which plans get put into action. Some candidates want to add thousands of new pedestrian streets and green belts, while others want to maintain existing green spaces and improve schoolyards. These efforts will shape how Paris adapts to climate change and improves life for its residents.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Paris’s shift toward more trees, fewer cars, and greener streets has gained broad political support, promising a healthier and more climate-ready city future.
articles.personality_title_fr: La transformation écologique de Paris reçoit un large soutien avant les élections
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Paris a beaucoup changé ces 25 dernières années, surtout sous des maires de gauche qui ont voulu rendre la ville plus verte et moins encombrée par les voitures. À l'approche des élections municipales, les candidats débattent de l’avenir de la capitale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Alors qu’autrefois les conservateurs s’opposaient aux politiques écologiques comme la réduction du trafic automobile et la plantation d’arbres, beaucoup soutiennent désormais des idées similaires. Paris a réduit ses émissions de carbone de presque un tiers et la pollution de l’air de moitié depuis 2004. Environ 150 000 arbres ont été plantés depuis 2020, et le nombre de voitures a diminué de plus de moitié. Les candidats de gauche, droite et extrême droite s’accordent en grande partie pour protéger et augmenter les espaces verts et rendre la ville plus agréable pour les piétons.

**Impact** – Ce large accord sur les objectifs environnementaux montre que les changements verts à Paris vont probablement continuer. Ces politiques aident à réduire la pollution, améliorer la santé et préparer la ville à des étés plus chauds dus au changement climatique. L’attention portée aux espaces verts améliore aussi la vie quotidienne en créant des lieux publics plus agréables et des rues plus sûres près des écoles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les prochaines élections détermineront quels projets seront réalisés. Certains candidats veulent créer des milliers de nouvelles rues piétonnes et des ceintures vertes, d’autres veulent préserver les espaces verts existants et améliorer les cours d’école. Ces efforts façonneront la manière dont Paris s’adapte au changement climatique et améliore la vie de ses habitants.

**Une phrase à retenir** – Le virage de Paris vers plus d’arbres, moins de voitures et des rues plus vertes bénéficie d’un large soutien politique, promettant un avenir plus sain et mieux préparé au climat.
articles.personality_title_es: La transformación verde de París recibe amplio apoyo antes de las elecciones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – París ha cambiado mucho en los últimos 25 años, especialmente bajo alcaldes de izquierda que han trabajado para que la ciudad sea más verde y tenga menos coches. Con las elecciones municipales cerca, los candidatos debaten sobre el futuro de la capital.

**Qué pasó** – Aunque antes los conservadores se oponían a políticas ecológicas como reducir el tráfico y plantar árboles, ahora muchos apoyan ideas similares. París ha reducido sus emisiones de carbono casi un tercio y la contaminación del aire a la mitad desde 2004. Se han plantado unos 150,000 árboles desde 2020 y el número de coches ha bajado más de la mitad. Los candidatos de izquierda, derecha y extrema derecha están mayormente de acuerdo en proteger y aumentar los espacios verdes y hacer la ciudad más amigable para peatones.

**Impacto** – Este amplio acuerdo sobre objetivos ambientales muestra que los cambios verdes en París probablemente continuarán. Estas políticas ayudan a reducir la contaminación, mejorar la salud y preparar la ciudad para veranos más calurosos por el cambio climático. El enfoque en espacios verdes también mejora la vida diaria creando áreas públicas más agradables y calles más seguras cerca de las escuelas.

**Próximo paso** – Las próximas elecciones decidirán qué planes se llevarán a cabo. Algunos candidatos quieren añadir miles de calles peatonales nuevas y cinturones verdes, otros quieren mantener los espacios verdes existentes y mejorar los patios escolares. Estos esfuerzos definirán cómo París se adapta al cambio climático y mejora la vida de sus habitantes.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La transformación de París hacia más árboles, menos coches y calles más verdes cuenta con amplio apoyo político, prometiendo un futuro más saludable y preparado para el clima.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of the Seine riverbanks transformed into vibrant green pedestrian promenades lined with newly planted trees and lush grassy cobblestone paths, with abstract silhouettes of cyclists and walkers enjoying the space, framed by elegant Haussmannian buildings and a large symbolic mural on the city hall façade reflecting environmental renewal.

articles.title: Scientists discovered a secret deal between a plant and beetles
articles.summary: A study from Kobe University has uncovered a surprising partnership between Japanese red elder plants and Heterhelus beetles. The beetles pollinate the flowers but also lay eggs inside the developing fruit. The plant responds by dropping many of those fruits, yet the larvae survive by escaping into the soil. The discovery suggests that fruit drop is not punishment but a compromise that keeps the plant–insect relationship stable.
articles.content:
Japanese red elder plants protect their own survival by dropping fruits that contain Heterhelus beetle larvae. Surprisingly, this action also allows the larvae to survive. A study from Kobe University suggests this unusual interaction reshapes how scientists understand the balance between plants and the insects that pollinate them.

In some plant insect relationships, the insect both pollinates the plant and uses the fruit as a place for its offspring to grow. Biologists refer to this type of partnership as "nursery pollination mutualism." Kobe University botanist Kenji Suetsugu explains, "These interactions are fascinating because they sit on the boundary between cooperation and conflict."

Classic examples include figs and fig wasps and yuccas and yucca moths. In these systems, plants often control insect populations by dropping fruits that contain too many larvae. Because the larvae typically die when the fruit falls, scientists have long viewed this process as a punishment that keeps the relationship balanced.

Suetsugu began to question whether this explanation applied to Japanese red elder plants. "I once observed Japanese red elder flowers full of Heterhelus beetles mating and feeding, and I also saw fruits infested by the beetles' larvae dropping in large numbers. With such seemingly great losses to both sides, I wondered whether this was really punishment and how the insects keep their losses contained," says Suetsugu, voicing suspicion that there is something missing in the current narrative of the sanction-driven balance in nursery pollination mutualisms.

Investigating the Plant Beetle Relationship

To explore this puzzle, Suetsugu and his colleagues focused on two key questions. First, are Heterhelus beetles essential pollinators for the Japanese red elder Sambucus sieboldiana? Second, what mechanism allows this relationship to remain beneficial for both species?

Suzu Kawashima, a master's student in Suetsugu's laboratory, describes the complex approach required to answer these questions. "To tackle this issue, one requires an unusual combination of careful field observation of pollination events, exclusion and hand pollination experiments, as well as developmental tracking of the insects even after the fruit drop. Many studies stop at one of these steps, simply because doing all of them takes time, patience and logistical commitment."

Fruit Drop That Protects Both Plant and Larvae

The research team reports its findings in the journal Plants, People, Planet. Their experiments revealed that the Japanese red elder depends on Heterhelus beetles for pollination. At the same time, the plant aborts nearly all fruits that contain larvae, which helps limit the plant's resource investment.

However, the larvae do not die after the fruit falls. Instead, they leave the dropped fruit and burrow into the soil, where they continue developing until maturity.

"What our finding shows is a different route to a stable balance, where fruit abortion can function as a compromise that both sides can tolerate. This finding shifts the narrative from dropping fruit as punishment to it being a shared benefit -- without denying the underlying conflict that defines nursery pollination mutualisms in the first place," says Kawashima, who was the first author of the study.

Environmental Factors Shape the Balance

The researchers also calculated the costs and benefits of the relationship between the plant and the beetles. Their analysis showed that this balance varies across locations, suggesting environmental conditions influence how the interaction works.

Kawashima explains: "While all Heterhelus beetle species depend on elder plants for reproduction, the same is not true in reverse, and there is considerable variation in pollinator dependence across elder plant species. In future work, mapping where Heterhelus dominates versus where alternative pollinators are more important should clarify the ecological drivers behind when the 'fallen-fruit compromise' is favored and when it is not."

Rethinking Cooperation in Nature

For Suetsugu, the findings highlight how cooperation in nature can arise from processes that initially appear wasteful or unsuccessful.

"On a personal level, this study makes me feel that we are only beginning to appreciate how much cooperation in nature is maintained by mechanisms that look, at first glance, like failure. A fallen fruit looks like a loss. Realizing that it can instead be the very structure that keeps a mutualism stable is exactly the kind of insight that makes me want to keep following these interactions year after year."

The research was funded by the Japan Science and Technology Agency (grant JPMJPR21D6) and carried out in collaboration with a researcher from the University of Human Environments.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about a unique plant-insect mutualism that reshapes understanding of ecological cooperation. The findings have broad implications for ecology and conservation, highlighting complex natural balances that benefit both species and potentially informing environmental management.
articles.personality_title: Scientists reveal surprising teamwork between Japanese red elder plants and beetles
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some plants and insects work closely together. In a special kind of relationship called nursery pollination mutualism, insects pollinate flowers and lay their eggs inside the plant’s fruit. Usually, if the plant drops the fruit, the insect larvae inside die. This was thought to be how plants control insect numbers.

**What happened** – Researchers at Kobe University studied Japanese red elder plants and Heterhelus beetles. They found that the beetles pollinate the plants and lay eggs inside the fruit. The plants then drop many of these fruits. But surprisingly, the larvae inside don’t die. Instead, they escape into the soil and continue growing.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists understand these plant-insect partnerships. Instead of fruit dropping being a punishment, it is a kind of compromise that helps both the plant and the beetles survive. The plant saves resources by dropping fruit with larvae, and the larvae survive by moving into the soil. This shows nature’s balance can be more complex and cooperative than previously thought.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to study how this relationship changes in different environments. They want to find out where this ‘fallen-fruit compromise’ works best and when other pollinators take over. This could help us understand how plants and insects adapt to changing conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Japanese red elder plants and beetles maintain a surprising balance where dropped fruit helps both species survive, reshaping our view of nature’s teamwork.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques révèlent une collaboration surprenante entre le sureau rouge japonais et les coléoptères
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certaines plantes et insectes travaillent en étroite collaboration. Dans une relation spéciale appelée mutualisme de pollinisation-nurserie, les insectes pollinisent les fleurs et pondent leurs œufs à l'intérieur des fruits. Habituellement, si la plante fait tomber le fruit, les larves d’insectes meurent. On pensait que c’était ainsi que les plantes contrôlaient le nombre d’insectes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Kobe ont étudié les sureaux rouges japonais et les coléoptères Heterhelus. Ils ont découvert que ces coléoptères pollinisent les plantes et pondent des œufs dans les fruits. Les plantes font ensuite tomber beaucoup de ces fruits. Mais, étonnamment, les larves ne meurent pas. Elles s’échappent dans le sol et continuent à grandir.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques comprennent ces partenariats plantes-insectes. Au lieu que la chute des fruits soit une punition, c’est une sorte de compromis qui aide la plante et les coléoptères à survivre. La plante économise ses ressources en laissant tomber les fruits avec des larves, et les larves survivent en se déplaçant dans le sol. Cela montre que l’équilibre dans la nature est plus complexe et coopératif qu’on ne le pensait.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier comment cette relation change selon les environnements. Ils cherchent à savoir où ce « compromis du fruit tombé » marche le mieux et quand d’autres pollinisateurs prennent le relais. Cela pourrait aider à comprendre comment les plantes et les insectes s’adaptent aux changements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le sureau rouge japonais et les coléoptères maintiennent un équilibre surprenant où la chute des fruits aide les deux espèces à survivre, changeant notre vision de la coopération dans la nature.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren una sorprendente colaboración entre el saúco rojo japonés y escarabajos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunas plantas e insectos trabajan juntos de manera especial. En una relación llamada mutualismo de polinización y cría, los insectos polinizan las flores y ponen sus huevos dentro de los frutos. Normalmente, si la planta deja caer el fruto, las larvas mueren. Se pensaba que esta era la forma en que las plantas controlaban la cantidad de insectos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Kobe estudiaron el saúco rojo japonés y los escarabajos Heterhelus. Encontraron que los escarabajos polinizan la planta y ponen huevos dentro del fruto. La planta deja caer muchos de estos frutos. Pero, sorprendentemente, las larvas no mueren. Escapan al suelo y siguen creciendo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia cómo los científicos entienden estas relaciones entre plantas e insectos. En vez de que la caída del fruto sea un castigo, es un compromiso que ayuda a la planta y a los escarabajos a sobrevivir. La planta ahorra recursos al dejar caer el fruto con larvas, y las larvas sobreviven moviéndose al suelo. Esto muestra que el equilibrio en la naturaleza es más complejo y cooperativo de lo que se pensaba.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar cómo cambia esta relación en distintos ambientes. Quieren saber dónde funciona mejor este ‘compromiso del fruto caído’ y cuándo otros polinizadores toman el relevo. Esto podría ayudar a entender cómo plantas e insectos se adaptan a cambios.

**Resumen en una frase** – El saúco rojo japonés y los escarabajos mantienen un equilibrio sorprendente donde la caída del fruto ayuda a ambas especies a sobrevivir, cambiando nuestra visión del trabajo en equipo en la naturaleza.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a Japanese red elder plant with softly glowing red fruits gently falling to the earth, where small, delicate beetle larvae emerge and burrow into the soil beneath, symbolizing a harmonious cycle of mutual survival and cooperation between plant and insect.
